content: The single most frustrating moment for any Rust systems programmer encountering the custom allocator problem is not when they fail to compile their code, but rather when their compiler silently accepts a broken implementation that behaves differently than expected on embedded platforms or in no_std environments. The fundamental mistake beginners make—specifically the one that trips up even experienced developers—is thinking that implementing the Drop trait for their heap data structure automatically satisfies the requirements of GlobalAlloc and #[global_allocator]. This misconception stems from a dangerous misunderstanding of what the allocator actually needs to do versus what Rust's ownership model enforces on the programmer. When you write your own allocator, you are not just building a container; you are acting as an intermediary between the operating system's raw memory pages and the high-level types that consume them, and if that contract is violated, your program will panic at runtime with no stack trace to guide you back to where you broke it. The core of this problem lies in how the #[global_allocator] macro interacts with the Drop trait. When you define a custom allocator using #[global_allocator], you are instructing the Rust compiler to manage memory for you by calling your drop function exactly once when the entire process exits, regardless of whether individual allocations within that heap were successfully freed. If your implementation relies on manually tracking every allocation and then attempting to free them during Drop, you will find yourself in a deadlock: the OS cannot reclaim your pages until all pointers are dropped, but your logic requires those pages to be available before you can complete the drop operation for the entire allocator instance. This creates an impossible dependency where the allocator must know which allocations have been freed to determine if it should release memory back to the OS, yet the Drop trait is invoked only after every single allocation has failed or succeeded, leaving you with no way to distinguish between a 'free' and a 'panic'. The solution requires shifting your mental model from tracking individual objects to managing a single atomic state for the entire heap. Instead of trying to track allocations in a Vec or HashMap that gets dropped prematurely, you must implement a bump allocator pattern where every allocation either succeeds by advancing an internal pointer and returning the new memory block, or fails immediately by returning null without ever touching the allocated region, ensuring that the Drop trait is guaranteed never to be called while memory management logic is still active. By decoupling the failure path from the success path in your custom allocator implementation, you ensure that if a specific allocation fails, it does not consume any of the OS's pages and therefore will never trigger the global drop operation prematurely. The key insight here is recognizing that #[global_allocator] assumes you have already solved the problem of tracking allocations; it does not provide mechanisms for you to track them. Your job is to build the bookkeeping yourself in a way that respects the one-drop guarantee, which means implementing your allocator with an internal pointer that monotonically increases and never decreases, alongside a state flag that indicates whether any allocation has ever succeeded. When Drop runs on this structure, it simply checks if that success flag was set; if it was, you release all pages to the OS, otherwise you do nothing because no memory was ever taken from the system in the first place. This approach eliminates the entire class of bugs where partial allocations leave the process with fragmented or orphaned pages that never get reclaimed, a silent killer for low-memory embedded systems and long-running servers. Finally, when wrapping your custom allocator in #[global_allocator], ensure you are not using any standard library types like Vec inside your heap state because they will attempt to free their internal memory on Drop, creating the very deadlock we discussed earlier. You must implement your own raw pointer management with explicit manual deallocation logic that only runs if and when necessary, completely bypassing the automatic cleanup of Rust's standard containers until the allocator itself is fully destroyed by the runtime. This rigorous separation of concerns between your custom heap state and the global allocator lifecycle guarantees that your no_std environment will never run out of memory due to a logic error in how you've implemented Drop, giving you a robust foundation for building truly portable systems code.
